problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem C (data): analyze the New York Times Wordle Twitter dataset (~359 daily records: date, contest number, word, number of reported results, number in Hard Mode, and the 1/2/3/4/5/6/X attempt-distribution percentages). Four tasks: (Q1a) explain and forecast the daily number of reported results out to March 1, 2023; (Q1b) determine which word attributes drive the Hard Mode percentage and the mechanism; (Q2) forecast the full attempt-distribution (1..X) for a future given word (EERIE) and discuss uncertainty/accuracy; (Q3) classify solution words by difficulty, identify which word attributes link to each class, and evaluate classification accuracy; (Q4) find other interesting features in the data. This paper is the team #2307946 Outstanding-award solution.
models_used: ARIMA(0,1,1) time-series model for forecasting daily reported-results count, selected via ADF stationarity test + first-order differencing, ACF/PACF order identification, R auto.arima, AIC comparison (4200.897 vs 4202.825 vs 4205.466), and Ljung-Box white-noise residual test
Rolling-window outlier detection: mean +/- 3*std over a 60-day window (30 days before/after), with linear interpolation to fill removed/erroneous points

A 17-feature word-attribute system (word_freq, num_vowel, num_repeat, part-of-speech, sentiment, Ortho_N, Phono_N, OG_N, BG_Sum, NPhon, SUBTLCD, OLD, PLD, Concreteness_Rating, Semantic_Diversity, Assoc_Freq_R123, NMorph) sourced from English Lexicon Project, SUBTLEX, NLTK, vaderSentiment, and published psycholinguistics datasets
LightGBM regressor (Q1b) on Hard Mode percentage using lag terms (lag-1 to lag-5 of pct itself plus lag-0..lag-5 of all word attributes) to expose the lag mechanism via feature importance

Multi-output regression for the attempt distribution: a per-target GBDT baseline (7 independent regressors) vs a Multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE) multi-task deep model with task-specific gating networks; MMoE won (MSE ~145)
CTGAN conditional-GAN tabular data augmentation (expanded 359 -> ~4000 rows, filtering synthetic rows whose 1..X percentages did not sum to ~100%) -- tried and rejected
OpenFE automated feature engineering on tabular data -- kept (small gain on MMoE)
K-means clustering (k=4 chosen via SSE elbow) on the attempt-distribution to create difficulty labels, validated by per-group average tries (3.574/3.951/4.326/4.800) and skewness
LightGBM multiclass classifier mapping word attributes -> K-means difficulty group (test accuracy ~70-71%, F1 ~69%)
Box-Pierce white-noise test on each of the 7 attempt-percentage series to justify cross-sectional (non-time-series) modeling for Q2
key_techniques: Two-stage cluster-then-classify design: K-means generates unsupervised difficulty labels from the attempt distribution, then a supervised LightGBM maps interpretable word attributes onto those labels -- this answers both 'group by difficulty' and 'which attributes link to each group' with one pipeline

Lag-feature engineering to model a hysteresis hypothesis: feeding lag-1..lag-5 of word attributes AND of the Hard Mode pct, then reading feature importance to show pct's own lags dominate and same-day word attributes are nearly irrelevant -- because players cannot see today's word before choosing a mode (a causal, mechanism-level argument, not just correlation)

Rigorous ARIMA pipeline: ADF test (stat -1.96, p=0.59 -> nonstationary), differencing, a documented regime change on 2022-05-16 where variance drops so only the post-date stationary segment is used, ACF-truncated/PACF-tailed reading -> ARIMA(0,1,1), cross-checked against auto.arima and disambiguated by AIC, then residual white-noise validated

Honest negative results reported as findings: CTGAN augmentation made both models WORSE (added noise on a tiny 359-row dataset) and OpenFE helped only MMoE -- the paper keeps these in the narrative rather than hiding them

Importing external domain knowledge: grounding word difficulty in psycholinguistics (orthographic/phonological neighbors, bigram sum, semantic diversity, concreteness) from the English Lexicon Project and cited literature rather than inventing ad hoc features
Model-appropriate validation per task: MSE/RMSE/SMAPE for regression, Accuracy/Precision/Recall/F1 for classification, SSE elbow for k, white-noise tests to justify modeling choices

Sensitivity analysis matched to each model: perturb top-5 attributes by [(1-p)x,(1+p)x] and check importance-ranking stability for Q1; randomly delete 1-5 samples x100 and count unchanged classifications (>=90/100) for Q3
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, it wins on breadth-with-rigor plus interpretation. It deploys a coherent ladder of models (ARIMA -> LightGBM -> MMoE -> K-means+LightGBM) each justified by a statistical test, not chosen arbitrarily. Crucially it answers the 'why/mechanism' parts judges weight heavily: the hysteresis explanation for Hard Mode (past-word difficulty lowers confidence; today's word is unknown at choice time) is a genuine causal story backed by feature importance, and the cluster-then-classify design directly produces the per-class attribute drivers Q3 asks for. It demonstrates modeling maturity by comparing GBDT vs MMoE and honestly reporting that data augmentation FAILED on the small dataset -- a sign of real experimentation, not cherry-picking. It validates everything (ADF, Ljung-Box/Box-Pierce, AIC, SSE elbow, full classification metrics) and runs targeted sensitivity analyses. Presentation is professional: clear summary with concrete numbers, a model-overview flowchart, notation table, and a polished closing memorandum to the NYT editor with actionable business recommendations -- exactly the consultant framing Problem C rewards."
section_highlights: "Summary gives concrete quantitative results up front (ARIMA 80% PI [10139.23, 30808.07], MMoE MSE 145, EERIE distribution, classifier ~70% accuracy, EERIE -> Group 2). Section 2 has a one-glance Mermaid model-overview flowchart mapping each sub-problem to its models. Section 3 documents reproducible preprocessing (rolling 3-sigma outlier rule, a full outlier/misspelling checklist table, removal of non-5-letter words). Section 5 + Table 3 build the 17-attribute system with explicit meanings and data sources -- the analytical backbone. Section 6's feature-importance bar chart visually proves pct lags dominate over word attributes. Section 7 transparently narrates the GBDT-vs-MMoE comparison and the failed CTGAN augmentation. Section 8 ties K-means difficulty groups (Table 6 with avg-tries and skew) to LightGBM attribute importance (BG_Sum, num_repeat, Semantic_Diversity, PLD, Phono_N) and reports full Accuracy/Precision/Recall/F1. Section 10 provides per-model sensitivity tables. Section 12 is a client-facing memorandum with three operational recommendations."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The forecast question is internally inconsistent: prose targets March 1, 2023, but the result text and Figure 7 quote March 31, 2023 [10139.23, 30808.07] -- a date mismatch that should have been caught

The Q1b LightGBM reports implausibly tiny errors (validation MSE 3.38e-5, SMAPE 0.069) that strongly suggest leakage -- lag-1 Hard Mode pct trivially predicts today's pct because the series is smooth, so 'the model fits very well' overstates real explanatory power
Self-reported regime break at 2022-05-16 to get stationarity is somewhat ad hoc; discarding all pre-date data to satisfy ADF weakens the early-period model and is not strongly justified

Many embedded chart tables in this markdown render are clearly OCR/transcription-garbled (e.g., the Figure 12 series, PACF values climbing monotonically to 1.0, the correlation heatmap labels), so quoted figure numbers should be treated cautiously -- the modeling logic is sound even where the digitized tables are not

MMoE (a deep multi-task net) is trained on only ~359 samples, exactly the small-data regime where it is least reliable; the authors acknowledge they could not fully overcome the small-data problem and that CTGAN augmentation backfired
Assumption that the Twitter reported-results count equals the true player population and that reporters' Hard Mode share equals all players' is a strong, unverified selection-bias assumption
Word-attribute features depend on external databases (English Lexicon Project, SUBTLEX) whose coverage of every Wordle answer is not verified; missing-value handling is not discussed
Weaknesses section is thin -- it omits the leakage risk and the date inconsistency, the two most important real caveats
reusable_patterns: Cluster-then-classify template for any 'group by X and explain the grouping' task: unsupervised clustering (K-means + SSE elbow) on the outcome to manufacture labels, then a supervised interpretable model (LightGBM) from features to those labels, reading feature importance for the explanation

Lag-feature + feature-importance recipe to argue mechanism/causality from tabular time-indexed data: include lags of both the target and the drivers, then use which lags matter to make a temporal/causal claim (here: hysteresis)

Disciplined ARIMA checklist worth copying: ADF -> difference -> inspect variance for regime changes -> ACF/PACF order ID -> cross-check auto.arima -> AIC select -> Ljung-Box residual test -> report PI not just point estimate

Model bake-off framing: pit a simple ensemble (GBDT) against a fancier model (MMoE) on the SAME metric and let the validation gap decide -- and report failed improvements (CTGAN, OpenFE) as evidence of due diligence
Build a literature-grounded feature system with an attributes table (notation | meaning | data source) instead of ad hoc features -- instantly raises perceived rigor and reproducibility

Per-model sensitivity analysis patterns: multiplicative perturbation [(1-p)x,(1+p)x] with a stability threshold for feature-importance robustness, and repeated random row-deletion (x100, count unchanged outputs) for classifier robustness

Close a Problem C paper with a one-page client memorandum that restates each answer in plain numbers plus concrete operational recommendations -- matches the 'consultant to a real organization' framing graders reward
Rolling-window 3-sigma outlier rule + linear interpolation as a defensible, easy-to-document data-cleaning step, paired with an explicit outlier/correction checklist table
